item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously creepy, the moments of lucidity really seal this for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When sid from ice age is done with everyone’s bullshit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i gotta be honest. the picture made me laugh. I'm sorry, I'm not trying to be mean. most people seemed to love it so I'm clearly the odd one out and that's ok.

but as i read this, i was more and more… horrified.
I'm disabled, and I'm in a lot of pain. this will be my lot in life forever. to an extent, I sometimes feel like I'm a prisoner of my crappy body.

this gave me some perspective lol. it can always be worse!

this is one of few times I was rooting for the foundation to kill people — because that absolutely sounds like hell. I'd beg for death, too. that's some Johnny got his gun stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed this too, and seeing your comment gave me the impression of consensus that it was a mistake. I edited the article and added the missing dash. Hopefully this wasn't out of line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That thing is pure evil. Body horror is the most terrifying thing for me.

Very relateable for MS (Multiple Sclerosis) patients.

Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This anomaly is horrifying and intriguing down to the strikedthrough sentences in the article.
The only thing I noticed is that you forgot to put a dash in-between the last reference to "Subject-9225".

[Subject-9225 jerks his head back. Dr. Chung jumps back in her chair as Subject-9225 convulses, and security rushes into the room. Subject 9225 looks back to Dr. Chung with a blank expression.]

Other than that, outstanding concept and well-written.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing that bugs me is the number of victims in the last dropdown. I don't mean it's to fucked up, I mean it stretches plausibility that 6,000 people in a single location at a single time can go missing and nobody notices.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Terminating those poor people have been one of the most merciful things the Foundation has ever done. Great concept. Chilling interviews. A great SCP article in general.

+1
I actually didn't upvote this it was just the sloth controlling me anhbjdsdsj .442 fikv

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quick question regarding the color blind part, is it only related to deuteranomaly or does it also relate to protanomaly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops, fixed it. I might have been a few years late on noticing that mistake, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 from me, dawg. Just a small thing I noticed; in the description you have Procedure-XXJ9 while in the Addendum you have the procedure listed as Procedure-XXP9

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it possible that this SCP was the inspiration for the film "Get Out"?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It just sounds too much like "Ye olde thing that controls people."
No vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The article seems very well-written and the image is spooky - but I just don't seem to get any reaction out of it. I think maybe because of the concept of a 'sloth' being less scary and rather cute to me, but it just doesn't suit me. I can see why others find it scary, but personally, the article didn't garner too much of a reaction out of me.

No-vote for now, perhaps I'll re-read in the future and change my mind.

item.title: This is a good-ass article
item.content:encoded: It's fuckin spooky & it's got a sloth. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I prefer the old image, though I'm not sure why. Hmm..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to hear!! That was the goal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Glad it's getting a positive response, I figured it would be a good first step into contributing to the wiki!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, yeah that new picture is creepy af… gonna have nightmares for the next 40-100 hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm loving the new image. In fact, I think it's actually better than the old image.

Great stuff.

item.title: Re: Image
item.content:encoded: Presumably not; I deleted it anyway. Anyone can edit that sort of thing; it's not against the rules.

item.title: Re: Image
item.content:encoded: Er, you missed the rest of the image code. Is that intentional, or?

item.title: Image
item.content:encoded: The picture of the sloth is coming down for now. I found it on this site along with many other pictures that were deemed free use and unaccredited, not doing the proper research to find out that the owner is Anna C Weber. I apologize for this, both to the owner of the image and to everyone else who saw it and didn't know who the owner of it was. If I get permission it will go back up. Once again, very sorry about all this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, you did the impossible and made a guy in a sloth costume scary. Congratulations and +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great image, great skip.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just looked it up yesterday, was a fun video.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was definitely a good spook. That puppet/costume is just unnerving…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just letting you know that you made it on Nightmind's SCP Vault video.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've found myself wanting to put my life on autopilot several times now. Thinking, what if I could sit back and handle this boring task just using muscle memory.

That's why this SCP scares me.

Also, it would be good if you focused more on the effigies and "It controls us" part.

Anyway, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I haven't read anything on this sit (or in general, come to think of it) in a while that was actually scary, and it was a dude in an adorable sloth costume that did it for me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you did an excellent job creating that feeling of being trapped in your own body, with no control. It's very scary for that reason.

Also that picture of a sloth… it's one of those things where you are looking at something that you know could harm you badly, but it's editted ever so slightly so it doesn't. That fear of knowing how dangerous that photo is is something I wish more skips had. Kind of like that picture of 096 on that mountain where the pixels are blacked out.

All in all, great job. Truly scary. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this was well done. I don't always like articles where the Foundation kills innocent people but I think this a plausible example of the Foundation being overwhelmed by an anomaly, and also it may have been the best case scenario for the victims.

(Also I removed an extra dash on a crossed out line.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The information in the footnote you're referring to, which says to read the addendum, isn't about adding procedures but rather amending one at a higher clearance level. It justifies and explains the procedures in place that level 3 and up know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the change to the containment procedures be in the containment procedures section, rather than having a footnote in the containment procedures that says to read a section in the body of the article? Isn't the rule that everything you should need to know for containment should be in the containment section?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My main observation here is that this that the image is pretty central to this article, and I'm not sure how much I'd like it if it wasn't there. There's a few great creepy touches in the interviews- 'I think it wants us to act like it' and 'when I sleep, it makes me keep my eyes open' stood out to me. But I do agree the interviews have too much fluff, go on for too long, and could probably stand to be consolidated into one document, and the decision to cull off the victims at the end is a pretty standard device nowadays. Ultimately, what makes the article linger in my mind is the central image of the sloth photobombing a live video feed, and harassing its victims.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Been a fan forever. Glad to see it turned out so well (:

item.title: Re: i will defend this hill to the death
item.content:encoded:
1. That's like, your opinion man. No way around that

2. Is it intended for a layman audience? I suppose. I don't believe that should restrict people from using terms that aren't common knowledge. After all, why are there footnotes otherwise? What's the point of having the option to use it if it's frowned upon to include? I'll stick to my guns here as well and state that as long as using footnotes is an available tool we can use, that I support the use of them. It gives the writer more freedom to incorporate terms that I believe add just a bit to the article. Once again, I guess that's just an opinion. Just to be clear, I personally like when I see footnotes

item.title: i will defend this hill to the death
item.content:encoded:
1) I find needless exposition significantly more immersion breaking than having to google a word.

2) If it's intended for a layman audience, use layman terms. From an in-character perspective it's completely pointless to have both, since you're just saying the same thing twice, with nothing gained. Going back to the medical note comparison, if I'm writing something to give to a non-medical person (like a soldier's commander), I'll just use the layman term instead of trying to teach a grunt a new SAT word. From an out-of-character perspective it's… equally pointless. The reading experience is not enhanced by having both, or harmed by just having the common term. It's basic prose writing- don't use two words when one will do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I tend on the side of adding footnotes to explain stuff when necessary. The whole point is to keep the flow of the article going. If the person has to google a term, they've distracted themselves from the immersion of the article.

Besides, this isn't a super scientific document where everyone involved is a scientist who knows what all the terms mean, an SP article is more of a summary of the object that can be given to starting researchers, security guards, etc.

EDIT: Just to be clear, I'm saying I think you should keep the footnote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it's a big deal I can get rid of the footnote. It's not supposed to be an insult on the readers intelligence, just something to save them time in case they don't know what it is. I have the damn color blindness and I didn't know what the word meant until a month ago. Guess it depends on the person looking at it, but it's meant to be convenient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interviews drag on far too long without enough payoff.

Also,

<rumetzen> little things in scips that bug me: using a technical word, followed by a footnote explaining the meaning of the technical word
<rumetzen> like fuck, at least trust that your reader is smart enough to use google
<rumetzen> like if i'm writing a medical note i don't go "the patient had a stage 3 ulcer on his gluteal cleft (ass crack)"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is a little boring, but the interview logs add a lot of depth and horror. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Idea is fairly plain, but the interviews are pretty good and sell the danger moderately well. Given the premise, it's better than it should be, but there's not enough of a hook in the concept for me to upvote. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice job dude.

Solid upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the caption you put for the picture so that it would make sense in-universe to have it in the article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is gud.
 
– hide block

Researcher is empathetic, but not excessively so; effect is intriguing as to what this sloth wants, but doesn't make it blatantly obvious. Good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it in chat, liked it here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this idea from the word "go." Glad you persevered. Bravo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, I'd like to thank the practical thousands of people who helped me make this! Big thanks go to JackIke who made the image grey for me, Sibyl who held my hand along the way, Thekillerax for sitting with me for an hour to fix all the mistakes, and really so many other people. Hope y'all like it!

EDIT: There was a lot of stuff going on with the old image which resulted in me taking it down, but the good news is that there's a new one! Huge thanks goes out to VeryLargeArray, also known as Magoo1125 on the wiki, for making this amazing picture!
